claim: The cited design procedure selects a controller structure such as P, PI, PD, or PID before tuning that structure's coefficients.
expected_outcome_rationale: The chapter explicitly describes structure selection followed by coefficient tuning.

claim: The cited PID design procedure requires coefficient tuning to be completed before the controller structure is selected.
expected_outcome_rationale: The reversed ordering conflicts with the stated two-step procedure.

claim: Knowing only that a PID-family controller will be used is enough to determine its exact gain and time constants.
expected_outcome_rationale: The plant, performance objective, and tuning measurements are absent, so exact coefficients cannot be determined.

claim: For the cited polynomial-reference setting, zero steady-state error requires the open-loop integrator degree to exceed the reference-signal degree.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source gives the integrator-degree condition under the stated final-value assumptions.

claim: In the cited polynomial-reference setting, zero steady-state error is independent of the open-loop integrator degree.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source directly relates zero steady-state error to integrator degree.

claim: The exact steady-state error can be calculated without the plant, controller, reference type, or open-loop gain.
expected_outcome_rationale: The required model, input degree, and gain data are missing.

claim: In practical process control, the derivative term is often disabled because measurement noise can make it undesirable.
expected_outcome_rationale: The chapter notes that practical controllers often switch off derivative action due to measurement noise.

claim: Adding derivative action inherently removes measurement noise from every feedback loop.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source treats measurement noise as a reason to disable, not a property automatically removed by, derivative action.

claim: The optimum derivative time can be selected without a plant model, noise spectrum, sampling rate, or performance target.
expected_outcome_rationale: No information needed to tune derivative action is supplied.

claim: Settling time is the time after which the output remains within a specified tolerance band around its final value.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source defines settling time by remaining inside a tolerance band.

claim: Settling time is the first instant at which the output crosses its final value, even if it later leaves the tolerance band.
expected_outcome_rationale: A transient crossing does not satisfy the cited remain-within-band definition.

claim: A response's exact settling time can be inferred from its maximum overshoot alone.
expected_outcome_rationale: Overshoot alone does not provide the complete time response or tolerance crossing history.

claim: Closed-loop step responses may be classified as underdamped, overdamped, or critically damped.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source distinguishes the three named response classes.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF page 18 (book page 6), Figure 2 discussion

claim: Underdamped, overdamped, and critically damped responses are identical response classes with no dynamic distinction.
expected_outcome_rationale: The cited discussion presents them as distinct response shapes.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF page 18 (book page 6), Figure 2 discussion

claim: The damping class of an unspecified closed-loop system can be determined without poles, damping data, or a response trace.
expected_outcome_rationale: No system dynamics or observed response is supplied.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF page 18 (book page 6), Figure 2 discussion

claim: A model-free PID tuning method may use a small set of characteristic plant parameters instead of a complete plant model.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source describes using two to four characteristic parameters in place of a full model.

claim: Every model-free PID tuning method requires a complete first-principles mathematical model of the plant.
expected_outcome_rationale: Requiring a full model contradicts the cited model-free approach.

claim: Exact PID coefficients can be computed from the phrase 'model-free tuning' without any measured characteristic parameters.
expected_outcome_rationale: The characteristic relay or response measurements are absent.

claim: Sustained constant-amplitude oscillation under increasing proportional gain can identify the critical gain and oscillation period used by relay-style tuning rules.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source identifies critical gain and period at constant-amplitude oscillation.

claim: The critical gain in the cited tuning procedure is defined only after the process oscillation has decayed completely to zero.
expected_outcome_rationale: A fully decayed response conflicts with the sustained-oscillation condition.

claim: The critical gain and period of an unspecified SISO plant can be known without a relay experiment or response data.
expected_outcome_rationale: Neither the experiment nor its measurements are supplied.

claim: First-order-plus-dead-time models are used for processes including chemical, thermal, and manufacturing systems.
expected_outcome_rationale: The chapter explicitly lists thermal systems among FOPDT applications.

claim: A first-order-plus-dead-time model can never be used to represent a thermal process.
expected_outcome_rationale: The categorical exclusion conflicts with the cited application list.

claim: The numerical gain, time constant, and delay of a thermal plant can be derived solely from the word 'thermal'.
expected_outcome_rationale: Application category alone does not identify model parameters.

claim: In the cited family of integral error criteria, n equals 0 for ISE, 1 for ISTE, and 2 for IST2E.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source maps the three values of n to ISE, ISTE, and IST2E.

claim: Integral squared error is the criterion in that family with a squared-time weighting corresponding to n equals 2.
expected_outcome_rationale: The claim assigns the n=2 weighting to ISE, contrary to the source.

claim: The best error criterion for an unspecified plant can be selected without an objective, constraints, or disturbance model.
expected_outcome_rationale: No design objective or plant conditions are provided.

claim: Increasing open-loop phase or gain margin moves the relevant crossover point away from the Nyquist critical point at minus one plus zero j.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source describes larger margins as moving crossover points away from the critical point.

claim: Increasing open-loop phase or gain margin necessarily moves the relevant crossover point toward the Nyquist critical point.
expected_outcome_rationale: The direction toward the critical point is the opposite of the cited geometry.

claim: The exact gain margin and phase margin can be calculated from settling time alone without an open-loop frequency response.
expected_outcome_rationale: Frequency-response crossover data are required for numerical margins.

claim: In the cited digital control structure, converters perform sampling and hold operations with sampling period h.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source explicitly assigns sampling and hold operations to the converters.

claim: The cited digital control structure operates without any sampling or hold operation.
expected_outcome_rationale: Removing both operations conflicts with the described digital control structure.

claim: The numerical sampling period can be inferred from the fact that an analog-to-digital converter is present.
expected_outcome_rationale: Converter presence does not specify the selected period.

claim: For the cited continuous state-space model, exact discretization uses Ad equal to the matrix exponential of A times h and Bd equal to the integral of the matrix exponential times B.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source gives the matrix-exponential and integral formulas.

claim: Exact state-space discretization always sets Ad equal to the scalar product A times h.
expected_outcome_rationale: Replacing the exponential with A times h is not the cited exact formula.

claim: A numerical discrete state matrix can be calculated without knowing A or the sampling period h.
expected_outcome_rationale: The continuous matrix and period are required numerical inputs.

claim: The Tustin approximation substitutes s with two over h times one minus z inverse divided by one plus z inverse.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source states the standard rational substitution.

claim: The cited Tustin transformation substitutes s with z divided by h and has no denominator term.
expected_outcome_rationale: The proposed substitution conflicts with the cited numerator and denominator form.

claim: Digital controller coefficients can be derived by Tustin transformation without the continuous controller or sampling period.
expected_outcome_rationale: Both the continuous transfer function and h are missing.

claim: Increasing the sampling period can degrade control quality, introduce output error, and eventually contribute to instability.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source reports degraded response for larger periods and warns of instability.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF pages 18 and 20 (chapter pages 6 and 8)

claim: Changing the sampling period cannot affect control error, stability, or performance indicators.
expected_outcome_rationale: The categorical no-effect claim conflicts with the cited observations and conclusion.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF pages 18 and 20 (chapter pages 6 and 8)

claim: The largest stable sampling period of an unspecified system can be determined without a model or experiment.
expected_outcome_rationale: A stability bound depends on the specific plant and controller dynamics.

citations.locator: Chapter 1, PDF pages 18 and 20 (chapter pages 6 and 8)

claim: An excessively high sampling rate may increase processor burden and can produce a discrete representation with poor numerical properties.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source identifies processor and numerical tradeoffs at high rates.

claim: An arbitrarily high sampling rate is always computationally free and numerically ideal.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source explicitly rejects the claim of universal cost-free improvement.

claim: Processor utilization at a chosen sampling rate can be calculated without the hardware, algorithm cost, or scheduler.
expected_outcome_rationale: The implementation parameters required for utilization are absent.

claim: The cited digital control design process includes analysis, modeling, stability analysis, synthesis, controller tuning, and discretization.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source lists all six activities, including stability analysis.

claim: Stability analysis is excluded from the cited digital control design process.
expected_outcome_rationale: The omission conflicts with the cited process.

claim: The completion of a digital-control design can be established without any model, stability result, controller, or discretization artifact.
expected_outcome_rationale: No design evidence is supplied.

claim: PI regulation is commonly applied to variables such as speed, position, flow, level, and temperature.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source lists temperature and flow among common PI applications.

claim: PI regulation is categorically unusable for temperature or flow control.
expected_outcome_rationale: The categorical exclusion conflicts with the cited examples.

claim: The exact PI gains for a temperature loop can be selected from the application name alone.
expected_outcome_rationale: Plant dynamics and performance requirements are missing.

claim: Under the cited continuous transfer-function convention, a system with at least one pole in the open right half-plane is unstable.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source defines a right-half-plane pole as an instability condition.

claim: A continuous transfer function with a pole in the open right half-plane is guaranteed asymptotically stable.
expected_outcome_rationale: The stability claim is the opposite of the cited condition.

claim: Stability can be decided from the transfer-function order alone without pole locations.
expected_outcome_rationale: Order alone contains no pole-location information.

claim: Every transfer-function pole is an eigenvalue of the state matrix, but an eigenvalue need not appear as a transfer-function pole in a nonminimal realization.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source states the one-way implication and its converse limitation.

claim: Every eigenvalue of a state matrix must always appear as a transfer-function pole regardless of input-output realization.
expected_outcome_rationale: The universal converse conflicts with the cited limitation.

claim: Whether an eigenvalue is hidden by an input-output realization can be decided without the B, C, and D matrices.
expected_outcome_rationale: Input-output reachability and observability information is absent.

claim: System identification builds mathematical models of dynamic systems from measured input-output data.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source defines identification in terms of measured input-output data.

claim: System identification forbids measured input-output data and can only use a complete first-principles derivation.
expected_outcome_rationale: The claim excludes the defining data source.

claim: A unique dynamic model can be identified from an unspecified data set with no excitation, sampling, or noise information.
expected_outcome_rationale: No data quality or experiment conditions are supplied.

claim: Relay feedback can induce a limit cycle whose behavior is used to extract process information.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source describes inducing a limit cycle to obtain information.

claim: The purpose of relay feedback identification is to guarantee that no oscillation or limit cycle occurs.
expected_outcome_rationale: Eliminating all oscillation conflicts with the stated mechanism.

claim: Process parameters can be recovered from relay feedback without observing the induced response.
expected_outcome_rationale: The response measurements needed for identification are absent.

claim: Relay-induced oscillations may be used to determine an ultimate gain and period before applying initial PID tuning rules.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source describes determining ultimate gain and period from induced oscillations.

claim: The cited relay procedure determines initial PID parameters without any oscillation-derived gain or period.
expected_outcome_rationale: The claim removes the measurements used by the cited procedure.

claim: The ultimate gain and period can be calculated from relay amplitude alone without the process response.
expected_outcome_rationale: Relay amplitude alone does not provide the full observed limit-cycle data.

claim: Open-loop and closed-loop identification are both recognized classes of system-identification method.
expected_outcome_rationale: The source explicitly distinguishes open-loop and closed-loop methods.

claim: All system identification is necessarily open-loop, so closed-loop identification is not a valid method class.
expected_outcome_rationale: The categorical denial conflicts with the cited classification.

claim: The preferable identification class can be selected without process stability, disturbance, noise, or operating-risk information.
expected_outcome_rationale: Method selection depends on system and experiment conditions not provided.

claim: A closed negative-feedback loop may include a state estimator that participates in both estimation and control.
expected_outcome_rationale: The cited chapter presents a closed negative-feedback loop with a state estimator.

claim: A state estimator can never be part of a closed negative-feedback control loop.
expected_outcome_rationale: The categorical exclusion conflicts with the presented architecture.

claim: The estimator gain matrix can be selected without a system model or the uncertainty and noise weighting information.
expected_outcome_rationale: The gain depends on model and weighting information that is not supplied.
